# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. just watched unforgiven (1992), what a truly great masterpiece it is.

**Score:** 80
**Body:** Spare me guys for not watching it earlier, better late than never! honestly, i always heard it was a classic, but I didn't expect it to hit this hard.

​it’s not your typical western where the good guy just rides in and saves the day. it feels way more real and gritty. The Clint Eastwood is incredible, and the way the movie looks at violence and "heroes" is actually pretty deep.

​if you like movies that take their time and have a really strong ending, you have to watch this. It lived up to the hype for sure.

​what did you guys think of the ending? no spoilers for others, but that final shootout was intense.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. They don't make movies like Unforgiven any more, especially Westerns. Partly because there aren't other actor/directors like Clint Eastwood with decades of acquired experience within that genre still alive and making movies. There will also never be another Gene Hackman, without him that movie wouldn't be half as great as it is.
  - Hard to disagree. Hackman’s performance was a masterclass in screen presence. It’s rare to see a director today with the restraint Eastwood had—he knew exactly when to let the silence and the shadows do the talking. They really don't make them with that kind of patience anymore.
    - He was also SUPER strict about any anachronism appearing on screen.  Tire tracks in the dirt, roads in the distance, antennae, etc will not be seen in Unforgiven.
2. It's even better if you have seen lots of other westerns. It tells uncomfortable truths most other westerns like to gloss over and not look at while still being an amazing western.
  - I think that's a whole subgenre of western.  Movies like Old Henry and The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance might also fit in here.
3. "Deserves got nothing to do with it."

One of Gene Hackmans most menacing characters.
4. This is my top favorite movie. I never get tired of watching it.  Eastwood and Freeman are great. I can see them as young guys. Neds backing William up all the time but hes afraid/admires him when they were younger. I love the slow transformation, you can gaige by how he treats his horse. As he slides closer to his old self, he slowly gets meaner and meaner to his horse. 

I never understood what the difference between good and bad actors was until I saw this movie.  

You need to find the 2013 version Kurasawa did in Japanese.  Its a near re-film using Samurai.  
  - I never noticed that detail about how he treats the horse—that’s a brilliant observation. It really highlights the 'slow burn' of the movie. I’ll definitely look for the Japanese version; the connection between Westerns and Samurai films has always been so strong. Thanks for sharing!
  - I had no idea there’s a Japanese remake. *Thank you*
5. Absolutely awesome movie, with great performances by Gene Hackman, Clint Eastwood and Morgan Freeman.

## 2. Why are there so few movies where the woman is the “monster” and still loved?

**Score:** 65
**Body:** I’ve noticed that in a lot of movies, we see the “monster”  loved by a woman. But I can barely think of examples where the woman is the one who’s strange, dark, unlikeable, or even “monstrous” in some way and still deeply loved.

It made me wonder… is it just rare in storytelling, or does it reflect something deeper? Like the idea that women have to be soft or attractive to be loved?

I’d really love recommendations of films that explore this where the female character is flawed, monstrous maybe even unsettling, but still desired or loved anyway.

Would love more suggestions or even thoughts on why this trope is so uncommon

Edit:- i really appreciate all the suggestions. but I want something where the woman is a beast and shes being loved ( opposite of Frankenstein)

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My assumption (which may not be accurate) as to why this particular example is rare is that most of the people behind the writing, directing, and producing of films have historically been men.  
  - I certainly don't think that's inaccurate assumption at all. 
    - I appreciate that.
2. Jakob’s Wife, starring Barbara Crampton as a newly turned vampire and her loving husband 

ETA: The Santa Clarita Diet show starring Drew Barrymore as a flesh eating zombie and her loving husband
  - I wish that had more seasons.
    - I can’t upvote this sentiment enough. 
3. Underworld. Spring (2014). Innocent Blood. Cat People. Siren. The Hunger. Species. Corpse Bride.

  
If you want to know why it's because guys are generally the ones writing scripts for, making, or signing yes on the productions of movies. And they like to believe that even if they were outwardly monstrous they could get the girl.
4. Titane 
5. Monster House
6. I think it’s because most movies are written by straight men and they’re more likely to write about monstrous men and the women who love them.  

## 3. Need to find a movie to make my roommate and I laugh

**Score:** 24
**Body:** this all started when my roommate and I decided to watch Nirvanna the band the show the movie, which neither of us enjoyed (sorry to those that do, it just wasn't for us). we proceeded to watch zombieland because my roommate wanted something that would actually make her laugh, but still she said it had only managed to get a few chuckles out of her. 

For April fools we tried the birdcage. again, maybe a few chuckles, but nothing major. 

it has now become my mission to find a movie that will have her bursting at the seams laughing. she seems find shocking things funny (said she wants something similar to the "blood santa" episode of always sunny). any suggestions?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Shawn of the Dead
  - Also Hot Fuzz
2. Tropic Thunder
3. Tucker and Dale vs Evil
4. My two go-to movies if I need a laugh are “Clue” and “So I Married an Axe Murderer.” 
  - Also, “Grandma’s Boy”
5. Drop Dead Gorgeous (1999).  Black comedy about a beauty contest in the Midwest.  She will love this if she finds shocking things funny.  

Also, Very Bad Things (1998) is another black comedy worth watching.
6. Walk Hard
7. Game Night. It was way funnier than I expected, really absurd and fast-moving. At some point it just keeps getting more ridiculous and it works.
8. Kingpin

## 4. Artistic and deep movies recommendations?

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Im looking to expand my watchlist and i wanna hear some of your best underrated recommendations. I want to find more deep, emotional and visually beautiful movies that feel like a blurry dream, something that will leave a mark on you

feel free to also leave ur favorite movies or something you think everyone should see atleast once in their lifetime, different genres too wont hurt!!



### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Anything from Andrei Tarkovsky, specifically The Mirror and Stalker. 

Three Colors trilogy, specifically Blue

The Double Life of Veronique 

Blind Chance 

Y Tu Mamma Tambien 

Roma 

Portrait of a Lady on Fire

The Conformist
2. Ran 1985 film Japan,  In the mood for Love 2000 film HongKong , Hero 2002 Chinese film, The Holy mountain 1973 Mexico, Le samurai 1967 France, Perfume: The story of a Murderer, Life of Pi, The Warrior 2001 Korean film, House of flying daggers 2004, Amelie,  Portrait of a Lady on fire 2019 , Skin 2008 , 
Documentaries of course: Virunga 2014 ( recommended highly) , Dahomey 2024
Chasing coral, The velvet queen , Honeyland ,Baraka, Rivers and Tide, 
  - Literally Gold 

Thank you for first having such a great taste in movies 

The other best part is the variety - french, african chinese ... 

I saw some of the trailers from your trailer and  immediately impressed and added to my watch list 

Truly lovely list buddy, thank you so much 
3. Once Upon A Time In America 
4. Curse of the Golden Flower (2006) blew me away w how gorgeous and colorful it was when I first saw it 

Crouching Tiger Hidden Dragon (2000) walked so Curse of the Golden Flower could run 

What Dreams May Come (1998) an afterlife love story where one of the main characters was an artist and the scenes drip with surreal interpretations of that art 

City of Lost Children (1995) Jean-Pierre Jeunet makes incredibly visually beautiful and interesting films. in fact 

Amelie (2001) might be more in line w with what you are looking for (another Jeunet joint)
  - Have you seen Hero? Multiple color changes with the mood of the story
5. BBCs Alice in wonderland 1986(?) it’s a fever dream 
6. The Fountain (2007)
7. melancholia
8. The Lovely Bones

## 5. Looking for movies like Annihilation

**Score:** 16
**Body:** I cant find any movies that are similar to Annihilation so if you have any reccomendations for a movie that have a similar feel to Annihilation. I’m talkkng about a movie thats kind of uncanny and has a feeling of doom and its uncomfortable to watch

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Color out of Space 

Aniara
  - Always recommend Color Out of Space because of the same idea of an unknown spacial anomoly causing all kinds of changes (seen & unseen). Plus it’s just a fun time & i’m a sucker for Lovecraft
  - Aniara was so good. I haven’t felt that existential dread since watching the German series Dark. 
2. Stalker

The Road
3. Under the Skin (2013).
4. Moon

Shallow Grave

Nocturnal Animals

Bug

Bones and All

Night watch (1997)






5. I found Men by the same director had that lingering unease. Maybe the weaker of the two films, but certainly worth watching…

Under the skin, and burning come to mind too as far as a films feeling like there’s a dark presence playing a role…
6. Titane.  Ex Machina.  Beyond the Black Rainbow.  The Lazarus Effect.  Possessor.  Banshee Chapter.  Vivarium. 
7. Try the Invitation. Very unsettling dinner party that takes a wrong turn. 
8. The Thing

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. I loved the 1999 movie “The Three Kings”. George Clooney, Mark Wahlberg and Ice Cube worked great together. The movie has a Hurt Locker indie type feel at times but also an adventure movie mixed with war and it does work surpisingly well by the end. If you like war movies this is a good watch.

**Score:** 732

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Gorge Clooney punched the director David O Russel in the face when the director started verbally attacked a PA (coffee runner) PAs can’t defend themselves on set, and Russel is one of the worst directors to work with. Even Jennifer Lawrence admitted her 3 films with him were great, “he taught me a lot, but I didn’t know at the time that wasn’t normal to be treated like that” 

Russel even had Christian Bale get in his face when he made Amy Adams cry 

Film is great but despise that director  
  - Many of his films are great - this, The Fighter, Silver Linings Playbook, American Hustle...I thought Joy was alright. 

But I do agree it's another instance of 'separating the art from the artist'.

Russell is just...he hasn't learned anything over the years. He's got a John Madden biopic with Nicolas Cage & Christian Bale due in November, and his Wiki already lists another alleged incident from May '25 on that set!


2. I told you Lexus makes a convertible.
  - Infiniti only
3. We three kings be stealing the gooold
4. I rigged the football with C4, sir. 
  - I don't really have a day job, sir.
5. It was good, one of the first new era war movies dealing with invading Kuwait.  
6. "Walter, just stand outside so Chief can translate my Iraqi ass map... okay?"

Still one of the funniest lines I've ever heard in a movie
7. Is this the movie with the hyper realistic portrayal of a tension pneumothorax?

## 2. What makes Quentin Tarantino’s dialogues so good?

**Score:** 477
**Body:** Is it the realism? The allegories? The irony?

From Pulp Fiction’s burger scene to The Inglorious Basterd’s intro scene, it is often said that Tarantino is the master of dialogues. I want to know what exactly makes his dialogues so unique and appealing. I was hoping some of you have any ideas?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The dialogue isn't plot driven
  - Yep, it’s character driven. He writes dialogue that shows more about the characters than the story.

Pulp Fiction dialogue feels so great because it’s what those characters would say, not what they need to say.
  - AND it still has substance. Most of the audience was probably not aware what the French called a quarter pounder with cheese. The whole conversation is centered around the small differences overseas compared to the US. 

It's also the implication. Most people surmise that Vincent was sent overseas to lay low because he had a lot of heat -- Tarantino later explained this was a connection to Reservoir Dogs and he intended on making a movie about Vincent and Mr. Blonde. 

So it feels like a real conversation that could happen, and there's a deeper story connected with it. But it's not necessary to the plot. 

Lastly, it's basically a meme. Not saying Tarantino made it intentionally to become a meme but "royale with cheese", "five dollar shake", etc. were very quotable parts of the film. 
  - ![gif](giphy|XABTVorVODddu)
2. The fact that most of it feels like a conversation that would be had in the moment in time. 
  - I really liked the opening scene for Reservoir Dogs. The best part is the way they debate tipping is how they each go out!
    - The Madonna conversation is a regular in my house hold 
3. I love every moment of PF… but something hits clear and carefree with Vince and Jules in the car
4. There’s a rhythm to them, and he’s meticulous about getting the right people to speak them. On paper, no one would ever talk like a Tarantino character, but once they’re spoken live they just feel so natural and fluid. 

To preface the following analogy, in NO WAY am I remotely comparing the two, but it’s like a Shakespeare play. Reading one is one thing, but a talented actor who has a solid grasp of the script makes the words come alive in a really special way.
5. Feels like a conversation, rather that law dumps and exposition

## 5. Annihilation (2018) is a fascinating movie that should've been a book

**Score:** 263
**Body:** And yes I do realize the movie was based on a 2014 book with the same name. From what I’ve heard, though, the movie feels more inspired by the book rather than a direct adaptation, with significant changes to the story.

This is not an exciting movie - I was bored for at least 70% of the runtime. But at the same time, it was infinitely fascinating and thought-provoking in a weird way. The heavy use of symbolism and metaphor, especially around self-destruction, environmental change, and the way identity evolves, makes it feel less like something meant to be watched for engagement and more like something meant to be interpreted and reflected on. A lot of it ties into ideas like cancer, trauma, and personal growth - that change, while terrifying and transformative, is also inevitable - which is why it feels better suited to a book.

Usually, this kind of reaction only happens when I watch a movie that doesn’t immediately impress me or just feels fine at first. I might walk away thinking it was a bit underwhelming, but over time what sticks with me are the themes and the meaning behind it. Annihilation is one of the strongest examples of this - I’ve never enjoyed a movie more after watching it than while actually watching it.

A big part of that comes down to how the film is executed. The dialogue is kinda stilted and awkward, and the pacing is often unnecessarily slow, especially with how silent and drawn-out many scenes feel. The last 30 minutes are also confusing, to say the least. These things make it a difficult watch, even though they don’t take away from what the movie is trying to say.

That’s why I think this concept works better as a book. The environment, the creatures, the scenery, that weird alien LSD mind-trip - all of it feels like it would benefit from detailed description and the reader’s imagination. Instead of being limited by slow visuals and awkward dialogue, it could lean fully into its ideas about self-destruction, trauma, and the constant reshaping of identity. Even the character deaths feel poetic and intentional in a way that I associate more with thematically rich books.

I would miss the music and some of the visuals, but overall it feels like something that’s more “food for thought” than an engaging watch. A weird comparison, but The Great Gatsby felt similar to me - mostly just events unfolding to set up symbols and metaphors that pay off in the end.

I am really struggling to find a fitting score for this movie. I just finished it, so I still remember how unimpressed I was for most of the runtime, yet I keep thinking about it and stewing in its ideas. I think I’ll settle on a 3.5/5. The execution leaves a lot to be desired, in my opinion.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Movie based on a book... should have been another book?
2. Slow burn, mindfuck movie. Perfect. I avoided it for some time because bad reviews, a to this day I saw it three times. Cosmic horror 👌
3. It's a good movie and a decent adaptation, in my opinion. It sounds like you just didnt connect with what the material was offering. 

Probably in my top 3 sci fi movies from the last 10 years. 
4. It’s based on a book. Good movie.
5. It has some actually awesome horror moments
  - There are a bunch of them but the footage they find of her husband’s special forces team was one of my favorites.
    - ScreamBear has entered the chat.
6. It's a great sci-fi movie 
7. awesome mindfuck of a movie. a healthy adaptation of the book, which I imagine was a crazy hard task given how cerebral the Southern Reach series is. I’m surprised by this take, perhaps it’s just not your style but this is one of those few adaptations that worked equally as well as the book itself 
8. "This movie could've been an email!"

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Train Dreams wants to have its cake and eat it too, regarding that first act change from the book

**Score:** 157
**Body:** So in the beginning of the movie, our hero Robert talks about how he appreciates how many races are coming together to get the work done. He then witnesses a few guys pick up a Chinese man and throw him off a bridge.

“What did he do wrong?” Robert yells, watching, shocked.

Later on in the movie, he gets visions of the Chinese man, feeling guilty for his wrongdoings (despite having done nothing, By all accounts he's always been as a good man). It appears later that the dead Chinese man has cursed him, and this is why Robert is suffering, why death follows him where he goes.

I turn to my girlfriend during all this and say, “Wait, did Robert help throw the man off the bridge? He didn’t, right? Why is the movie acting like he did?”

Robert didn’t do anything wrong. Perhaps he could have tried to help more, but this is not cause for cosmic revenge.

Later I look up the plot of the novel to find out he DID help throw him off the bridge, but the Chinese man escapes, and Robert sees him later and believes he is cursed by him.

The movie still wants to reap all of the benefits of this plot point, while not being courageous enough to have actually done it, so that we like Robert at the start of the movie.

I feel that the movie would have been so much better if it had happened, because it would inform all of the actions forward; his new friendship with the native, for instance, would land more if it’s in response to his guilt for what he had done to the Chinese man. His talk of guilt over wrongdoings would actually make sense, for another thing.

“Don’t you think it’s a bit much?” he asks the spirit of the Chinese man, when his wife and daughter have died in a fire. “What did they do to deserve that?”

But Robert didn’t do anything at all to deserve this curse. That happened in the book, not the movie. Why the hell is he being haunted by the spirit of him then?

All that aside, it’s a beautiful movie, with lyrical writing and wonderful shots that feel like a painting.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ok having not read the book but having seen the movie I did not AT ALL get that the curse was from the chinese guy, more like the world is hard and nobody cares.  I concur with your point, that would have been far more interesting.

His inaction is the entire lamentation of the film.  Not that he did anything wrong, just his passivity has resulted in his life passing by with him more as observer than captain
  - Yeah if this Chinese man was supposed to be the reason, real or perceived, I missed it.  To me, the story was a mix of the hardships of the hand he was drawn, mixed with regret that his life hadn’t turned out better because of both circumstance and choice. 
    - fwiw in the script they uploaded for awards contention it’s made pretty explicit that he’s regularly asking the Chinese guy why he’s doing this. So at least from the character’s perspective in the script he does seem to believe he’s cursed by this guy https://s3.documentcloud.org/documents/26467170/train-dreams-read-the-screenplay.pdf
    - This was my takeaway exactly.

EDIT: To add, I think this is also why he is compelled to help the girl that broke her leg. Yes, he thinks she may be his dead daughter but he doesn't have to regret not acting when he could have.
  - This is getting philosophical, but i actually liked the ambiguity of his action/inaction. Not doing anything, or not doing enough is shown to become this heavy moral burden and motor of guilt. I feel this might be a stronger message than the more run-of-the-mill “do evil, reap consequences”-trope.
2. He does grab the man at one point by the feet and it's unclear if he's trying to help him get free or help them carry him away. Based on later events, it may have been the latter. It definitely looks like it. 

But I also don't think he has to *be* guilty to *feel* guilty. I also don't think he was actually cursed, just plagued by guilt and blaming everything that happens on it. 
  - >He does grab the man at one point by the feet and it's unclear if he's trying to help him get free or help them carry him away.

It's been a couple of weeks since I watched it, but my impression was he wanted to help the Chinese man get free, but the man immediately kicked him because he was panicking, and thus the protagonist gave up right away.
  - I legit needed a double take cause I wasn't sure if it was him who grabbed the feet or a different person.
3. In the book Robert helps the rail workers throw the Chinese man off the bridge who then survives the fall, and Robert later feels cursed by it. But this is because Robert literally believes the Chinese man cast some kind of Eastern spell on him as they were dragging him to the bridge, not because he feels guilty for what he's done. The Robert in the book is a product of all the prejudices and lack of education of his times, and whatever guilt he does feel is tightly wrapped up in the racial stereotypes believed by a 19/20th century working class man. The movie has gone out of its way to sand down the rough edges of the past, presumably in order to make it palatable to a modern audience, but in doing so took away all that was interesting about the book and made the movie a hokey, Malick ripoff 
4. In the novella, the scene is different not only in Robert’s active cooperation, but also in the Chinese worker’s escape.  The film adaptation lets Robert off the hook with his puzzled half-hearted grapple, but the brutality of the execution gives the scene a horror and punch the story doesn’t have.  The scene is merely another episode of logging life in Johnson’s novella rather than a recurring episode of guilt.  I don’t blame the filmmakers for trying to give more cohesion to a relatively under-structured story.

## 2. How Carolco Pictures changed Hollywood cinema

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Carolco Pictures represented a unique era in Hollywood, a maverick independent studio that operated like a major, taking enormous creative and financial risks while giving unprecedented freedom to filmmakers like Paul Verhoeven and James Cameron. Carolco were also known for paying high salaries to their stars like Arnold Schwarzenegger and Sylvester Stallone. 

Founded by both Mario Kassar and Andrew Vajna, the studio achieved success with First Blood in 1982. The film was not only a success, but John Rambo became an icon of 1980s action, with Rambo First Blood Part II (1985) becoming another success, though Rambo III (1988) wasn't as successful as the first two. 

In 1988, Total Recall, based on Philip K. Dick's 1966 short story "We Can Remember It For You Wholesale" was rescued by Schwarzenegger, Kassar, and Vanja after being stuck in development hell for 14 years. Schwarzenegger chose Verhoeven to direct the film, having admired his film RoboCop. On a budget of $48-80 million, Total Recall was the most expensive film made in its time. Released on June 1, 1990, Total Recall became the fifth highest grossing film of the year, and the second highest grossing film of the summer behind Ghost. The success of Total Recall led to another Verhoeven Carolco collaboration with the controversial erotic thriller Basic Instinct (1992) with Michael Douglas and Total Recall co star Sharon Stone. 

But by far Carolco's greatest achievement was Terminator 2: Judgment Day. It was the most expensive film ever made at the time, with a budget of $94-102 million. Released on July 3 1991, Terminator 2 was a smash hit, grossing $519–520.9 million at the box office to become the highest grossing film of 1991 worldwide, and the third highest grossing film of its time behind Star Wars (1977), and E.T. The Extra-Terrestrial (1982). It is also seen as a major influence on visual effects in films, helping usher in the transition from practical effects to reliance on computer-generated imagery, with films like Jurassic Park (1993), and animated films like Toy Story (1995) becoming computer animated. According to industry professionals, Schwarzenegger became the top international star, ahead of actors such as Mel Gibson and Tom Cruise. It was inducted into the National Film Registry in 2023. 

However, despite Carolco's achievements, there was still problems. Kassar and Vanja parted ways in 1989. Despite Terminator 2's success, Carolco reported 1991 losses of $265.1 million, which was caused by the financial problems of its other films such as Air America, Narrow Margin, Jacob's Ladder, Chaplin, and Universal Soldier. They were suffering financially and needed the pirate adventure film Cutthroat Island to save them from financial hell. Unfortunately, Cutthroat Island became a box office bomb, ending Carolco for good. Carolco represents the best and worst of 1980s and early 1990s success. 

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 3. Why does Barry suddenly open up with people in Punch-Drunk Love?

**Score:** 8
**Body:** Punch-Drunk Love is one of my favourite movies so understanding every quirk of it and it's script mean a lot to me. 



In the film, one of the prominent thematic messages many others and I understood was the lesson of opening up our emotions to others to be given love and strength. The turning point for this happens around after Barry and Lena's first date. 

  


Barry is able to open up a bit to Lena by expressing his love and going to her after she shows that she still loves him even after his emotional tendencies were exposed by his sister. Barry however, still pulls away at the end and lies about no freaking out, still too scared too fully open up to her and embrace her love so the scene ends a bit disappointingly.

  


Then Barry is attacked by the scammers who have been chasing his for a lot of the movie and it's important to note here there's another instance of Barry trying to open up when he expresses his feelings about the situation to the goons, but they physically assault him for it. "Punishing" him for opening up.

  


The next day however, Barry seems perfectly fine opening up. He tells his coworker Lance about what he's been going through, dances in a supermarket and takes a trip to Hawaii to meet Lena where he can fully open up and embrace her love. 

  


My question is, what happened in between him pulling away at her apartment after their date and the morning after Barry is attacked that made him learn he had to be open with people?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lena popped Barry's cherry. He grew with her sister as the only boy (>!they mocked him all the time, the 'gay boy' joke that comes up as at some point!<), so much of Barry's demeanor is quite effeminate and self-repressed.

So he finally 'opened up.' Once his 'virginity' is lost, he finally feels like a man. He's know part of the real world. He deal with thugs and he's even attacked by them. Barry found a new meaning, a woman detached from the features that he despised from her sisters.

Lena disabused Barry of the idea about him being a weak man. Both his frailty and sensitiveness are what now make him strong.
  - I don’t remember Barry ever losing his virginity? Or do you mean not literally?

And also I still don’t get why Barry opens up. Like in his perspective, why would he do that? When does he learn it will be beneficial to him?
    - Yes, not literally (hence virginity in quotation marks), as he was able to be who he really was with Lena. Naked, you could say.

His perspective was anew. Lena struck him as the missing piece of his life. She empowered Barry, who was a very introverted everyman. It's not much what he 'learnt' as what he actually felt.

Lena was a rite of passage. Barry found meaning in her. He came apart with that lesser version of himself created by her sisters. *Punch-Drunk Love* does great at representing that irrational aspect of love. That's the 'suddenly' you're asking for.
      - But he needs to let Lena into his life. Like after their first kiss he still lies to her saying he doesn’t freak out for fear of her hurting him because of it like everyone else in his life. But then after he’s attacked by the scammers he’s able to open up to people, even talking to the random guy on the plane. What happens in between those scenes that changes Barry?
        - Barry still lying to Lena is a great little detail, as it yields realism to the movie (it would have felt off, like a day and night change).

I think that nothing really 'happens' other than Barry going through emotional shock and experiencing several previously unknown emotions to him.

Something just clicked between Lena, him facing the thugs and the thugs finally attacking him. Like some fragmented and much delayed catharsis.
          - So there isn’t really any change? He kinda just realised he needed to open up to Lena to feel loved. I’ve spent a week racking my brain over this I feel like I’m missing something. 
            - Maybe change isn't the right word. Self-disclosure and finally engaging with the world through someone, that's what happened to Barry. He committed to Lena as she acted as catalyst for his repressed emotions.

Now, if you're still not convinced, that's fine. You could be right and something is really missing.
              - I feel like my brain is going to explode. I love this movie to bits and I hate not getting this one piece of it. Thanks anyway
                - Maybe that’s what makes this piece of media so great? All great art that touches us will have some mystery to them, it’s more fun to accept it and let the emotions take over
        - He got beat up. It was like a point of catharsis.

## 5. What's the proper order to watch the various cuts of Apocalypse Now, I've never watched it before. And just got the 6 disc edition

**Score:** 3
**Body:** What is the proper order to watch the various cuts of Apocalypse Now, I've never watched it before, and I just got 6 disk edition..   Here's the listing of disks that i'm sure you're all familiar with. The original 1979 theatrical, Final Cut, Redux(extended cut), Hearts of Darkness.

Or I could just jump ahead and watch all of the bonus features and legacy content.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Theatrical cut first. Hearts of Darkness is the making-of documentary so make sure you’ve at least seen one version before it. Redux/Final Cut you can watch in any order, Redux is the longest (commercially available cut), Final Cut is the “middle ground” between it and the theatrical.
2. Honestly up to you, but I would start with the theatrical if you’re not sure. This is not a case where the theatrical cut is bad or chopped to bits. In fact, a lot of people prefer it.

Hearts of Darkness is fantastic but I would advise watching the film first.
3. Personally?
Having seen them all as they came out I'd switch the last two versions around and go:


Original, Hearts of Darkness, Final Cut, Redux. Definitely do not watch the bonus features before you've seen the film.


I've also seen the Assembly Cut. But that's pretty much unwatchable unless you're in film school and watching for practical purposes.
4. First time watching I'd definitely go with the Theatrical cut. Then Hearts of Darkness: A Filmmakers Apocalypse. If you feel like a rewatch eventually, go with redux, which is kind of a bloated mess. It has some interesting bits for sure, but the flow of the film is totally botched. Theatrical is the definitive edition imo. Final cut or whatever it's called is a less bloated version of redux, but it still destroys the flow of the film, so you might as well go with the redux and see it all. 
5. As someone who has adored the film since its release I'd go:

1) Final Cut -  It really is the most balanced version of the artists vision. It is the perfected version.

2) Hearts of Darkness  - This is invaluable for ones full appreciation for what was achieved. 

3) Theatrical Cut -  See what people  saw in 1979 and after watching Hearts of Darkness the respect that anything as brilliant as even the theatrical came together at all is astonishing. 

4) Redux - Even the director said it's too much of a great thing and just wanted to give everyone a chance to see basically everything. 

(Apocalypse Now was the film that made the young me see movies in a new light. Without having the vocabulary to express it I realized movies could be more than entertainment,  They were often the vision of someone called the "director" and could be inspired art instead of just entertainment. Jaws thrilled me, Star Wars wowed me, Close Encounters captivated me, Apocalypse Now enthralled and inspired me. I became a film nerd. ) 
6. I watched Redux and I liked it, but it definitely dragged. I think most people prefer the theatrical cut, so I suggest watching that. If you like it enough that you want some more stuff added, go ahead and watch the others, but some of what was cut is quite strange. 
7. Theatrical first. Theatrical last. The others have interesting bits but include scenes that ruin the pacing, most obviously the plantation sequence, which never should have been anything but a supplement. The theatrical cut remains the best. 
8. Start with theatrical, that's what I wish I'd done. I started with Redux and I agree with those that say it really throws off the pacing. The main additional section felt like a different movie and I found it really distracting.
9. I’ve watched all versions of *Apocalypse Now*, and it has confirmed for me that the theatrical cut remains the only one that counts. I don’t think the film gains anything of substance from any of the alternative decisions; on the contrary, the additional scenes often weaken the film and contradict the characters, with *Redux* being the worst offender of them all.

If you’re interested in the excess of the production, I encourage you to watch those versions for what didn’t make the final cut, much like one would watch extra features. But Coppola and the producers were on the right track not to include those scenes in the original cut.
  - Agreed. Part of the problem is that the non-theatricals include extra footage which was meant to be part of a sub-narrative which was never fully filmed, e.g. the bowman at the compound. So you get a bit of him with no context and no backstory. Just confusing and weird.

---
